title: iPhone 17 Pro Max Vs Samsung Galaxy S25 Ultra Gaming Test: Genshin Impact!
transcript: In this video, I’m conducting the iPhone 17 Pro   Max vs Galaxy S25 Ultra gaming test  by playing Genshin Impact on them. On the S25 Ultra, I’ve set the Screen resolution  to QuadHD+, the performance profile is set   to standard, and the game optimization  setting is also set to performance mode. I’ll be playing the game on the highest graphic  settings offered on both phones for 1 hour.   Genshin Impact offers 120 fps on the iPhone  17 Pro Max, whereas on the S25 Ultra,   it only offers 60fps. The settings are  overclocked on both phones. 17 Pro Max’s   battery is at 100% and the S25 Ultra is  at 98%. Now, let’s start the gaming test. The A19 Pro chip of the 17 Pro Max is  running the game very smoothly. The   FPS are staying above 100 even  during intense fighting scenes.   The Snapdragon 8 Elite of the S25 Ultra is  also running the game incredibly smoothly,   and the fps are staying at 60, which is  the maximum supported on this device. After about 15 minutes, the 17 Pro Max was  experiencing some fps drops. They ranged in   the 70s and 80s, but the game is still running  smoothly without any lag. On the other hand, the   S25 Ultra is still running the game at 60fps with  no fps drops. Although both phones have started to   heat up a bit. But as of now, I don’t notice any  significant performance throttling on either one. It’s been about 1 hour, and the iPhone 17  Pro Max is still running the Genshin Impact   very smoothly. During the fight scenes, the fps  ranged between 70s and 90s throughout the game.   Sometimes the fps went to 60s, but only for a  second; there were no major fps drops. Whereas   the S25 Ultra was running the game constantly at  60fps all the time. There were no frame drops,   not even a little. I’m sure if Genshin Impact  unlocked 120fps support for the S25 Ultra,   then it’d definitely perform just as  good as the 17 Pro Max, if not better. The 17 Pro Max’s temperature is over 43 degrees  after an hour of intense gaming, and its current   battery level is at 73. Meaning it consumed 27%  battery. The S25 Ultra’s temperature is also   over 43 degrees, and after 1 hour of gameplay,  its battery is at 68% meaning it consumed 30%   battery. And for full disclosure, the S25 Ultra  has been heavily used for the past 7 months,   whereas the 17 Pro Max is a fresh unit which has  been only used lightly for less than a month. So,   considering that, I’d say the battery drain  would be similar under the same conditions. So, to conclude, if you want the highest frame  rates available right now on Genshin Impact,   the iPhone 17 Pro Max has the upper hand.  But if 60 fps gaming is enough for you,   the S25 Ultra matches the iPhone in  stability and possibly could even   rival 120 fps performance if the game  unlocks it in the future. Both phones   handled heat and performance really well,  so the real difference here comes down to   fps support, not the hardware itself. And with that, I’ll wrap up this one. If you found this video informative,  then give it a thumbs up and subscribe   to Gizmo Rush for more cool tests  like this. Thanks for watching.

title: Xiaomi 17 vs. Galaxy S25 vs. Pixel 10 vs. iPhone 17 – Not Even Close!
transcript: ﻿The Xiaomi 17 has the largest battery you’ve  seen on a compact phone and a flagship camera   setup, and it’s the direct competitor to the  iPhone 17, which finally is using a ProMotion   display, the Pixel 10, which is the first base  Pixel phone with a triple camera setup, and the   classic Galaxy S25, which is the lightest phone  here. To see which one of these is the best choice   for you, I’m going to do a camera test with Maria  dressed up for Halloween. I’ll also do battery and   performance tests — and the result of the battery  test was shocking to me. Between the four phones,   the Galaxy is the lightest at only 162 grams  with a 4000 mAh battery, and it also has a   triple camera setup. The Xiaomi is only 191 grams  despite the fact that it’s packing a 7000 mAh   battery, and it also has a triple camera setup.  The iPhone 17 is also light — the battery capacity   is only 3692 mAh, weighing 177 grams. It doesn’t  have a telephoto lens and has only wide and   ultrawide cameras. The Pixel 10 is the heaviest at  204 grams since it has a large 4970 mAh battery,   and it also has a triple camera setup. The main  sensor size of the Xiaomi 17 is the largest,   the iPhone and Galaxy have a similar size, and the  Pixel has a rather small camera sensor for this   price tag. Therefore, when we take a picture, the  Xiaomi is going to have the most amount of bokeh   or background blur, followed by the iPhone and  Galaxy, and the Pixel has the least. So the only   phone that makes photos look like they’re coming  from a real camera is the Xiaomi, in my opinion,   since you get that juicy background blur that  you won’t get on other phones. That also affects   picture quality indoors, where the larger sensor  can gather more light. Also, the Xiaomi has two   main color modes — this one is taken in Leica  Authentic. It is a bit contrasty for my taste,   and for the sake of comparison, I use Leica  Vibrant in this video, and as you can see, the   colors are more typical of a smartphone. Also, for  reference, this is Maria dressed up for Halloween   in these specific autumn colors, and this video is  shot on a Sony full-frame camera. The Galaxy has   more neutral processing. The Xiaomi is increasing  the exposure on Maria’s skin, the iPhone white   balance is too warm, and the Pixel has a lot of  magenta. In two-times zoom, I can see the same,   but this time the Pixel also increased the  exposure on Maria’s face. And the observation   I mentioned stayed consistent throughout the video  for skin tones — the Galaxy, in my opinion, is the   most natural one, the Xiaomi is overexposed with  highlights sometimes missing, the iPhone is too   warm, and the Pixel too magenta. For other scenes  without people, the colors are closer. The Xiaomi   is the only one producing noticeably different  colors here due to the Leica color science.   Generally, Xiaomi colors are more vibrant  since we’re shooting in the Vibrant mode, but   as I explained, I also feel the Leica Authentic  mode is a bit too contrasty and dark. Overall,   though, all four phones are more than capable of  taking great shots in daylight, and computational   photography can also compensate to an extent  for the smaller sensor size. Especially if you   take photos of inanimate objects or plants, even  with higher ISOs like 500 here, the phones use   algorithms like denoising and produce a pleasing  final result. The same goes for video with the   main lens. Here I took 4K 30FPS videos. The Xiaomi  and Galaxy can also take 8K videos or 4K 120FPS,   while the Pixel and iPhone 4K are capped at  60FPS. All phones, of course, have portrait mode,   where the phone artificially blurs the background.  I do find the Xiaomi has the best processing for   Maria’s hair, followed by the Galaxy. The Pixel  has the worst, and the iPhone is alright but a   bit too aggressive in cutting her hair. The Galaxy  and Pixel telephoto lenses are only 10MP and are   small, while the Xiaomi telephoto is 50MP and much  larger, and the iPhone doesn’t have one at all.   The maximum amount of optical zoom you can get  looks like this on each phone, with the iPhone   having the least amount since you can’t zoom more  than 1x. If you zoom in 5 times with each phone,   you immediately notice the Pixel and Xiaomi have  better optical quality. The interesting part is   that the iPhone is not doing a bad job at all,  and you also get better background blur since   the main sensor is larger. I do feel Galaxy  should use a better telephoto lens next year.   The images you get with the telephoto lens  from buildings on the Galaxy can look decent,   but the Xiaomi is much sharper with more  details, the iPhone is rather disappointing,   and the Pixel is also of good quality. Also,  with the Xiaomi, you can zoom the furthest,   followed by the Galaxy, Pixel, and finally the  iPhone. For the ultrawide lens, the Galaxy,   iPhone, and Pixel have a similar focal length, but  the Xiaomi one is not as wide as the other three.   That means when you’re making a video, you can  fit less in the frame, but at the same time,   body proportions are going to look more natural  since a wider lens can distort them. I generally   find the Galaxy S25 not doing a great job  with lens reflection — when there’s sun,   there’s a mist on the photo, and shadows are  raised too much. I also noticed on the ultrawide   lens that the Xiaomi exposes the photo more than  the other phones, but nevertheless, both Xiaomi   and the others do great HDR processing. The  skin tone is also consistent on the ultrawide   camera across the phones, with the Galaxy being  natural, Xiaomi overexposed, iPhone warmer,   and Pixel leaning toward magenta. For the selfie  camera, the iPhone has a square sensor this year,   meaning you can take selfies both vertically  and horizontally. In vertical mode, the iPhone   and Pixel have the widest focal length,  Xiaomi is close to them, but unfortunately,   the Galaxy is too tight. On the iPhone, as I  mentioned, you can take vertical photos too,   which is really neat for content creation. We can  also check the selfie video quality together with   mic quality next! Now we are testing the selfie  camera. As you can see the Galaxy is too close,   so you cannot actually see Maria. The Xiaomi is  wide enough. The iPhone is also really wide, also   the selfie is square, so we can do a horizontal  or vertical video as you want. The Pixel is also   wide, but there is too much magenta, no? Yeah! Oh  hi! On this night photo with the ultrawide lens,   the Xiaomi has a totally different color science.  I find the iPhone did the best job, followed by   the Galaxy, but the Pixel didn’t process the  sky correctly. The same goes for the main lens,   where the Pixel’s sky looks banded. I find  Xiaomi’s processing better here, with the   photo being more exposed, likely due to the larger  sensor. The iPhone and Galaxy also did a decent   job. One interesting thing is that the Galaxy is  the only phone that doesn’t produce those weird   green light ghostings in photos. I wonder why the  other phones can’t just copy what Galaxy does. The   same goes for video, where on Xiaomi, iPhone,  and Pixel, you see these green dot reflections   at night. The Galaxy doesn’t have them, but its  quality is lacking. Xiaomi has a sharper result,   iPhone is acceptable, and Pixel also doesn’t  produce very detailed footage. The battery   capacity of the Xiaomi 17 is the highest, while  the iPhone has the lowest — but usually, iPhones   can last longer even with lower capacity. The  charging speed of the Galaxy, iPhone, and Pixel   are not that impressive, but at least the Xiaomi  17 has 100W charging speed. Also, on the Chinese   variant that I bought from. TradingShenzhen, the  charging brick is included. I put the link to the   phone in the description — I’ve bought countless  phones from these guys, and I 100% trust them.   They all support wireless charging, but the Galaxy  and Xiaomi do not have magnets, so you can’t snap   MagSafe or Qi2 power banks and accessories onto  the back. The iPhone has MagSafe, and Google has   Pixel Snap. For Galaxy and Xiaomi, you can, of  course, buy a MagSafe-compatible case and attach   the accessories that way. I’ll put the links to my  favorite accessories and cases in the description.   All four phones have a 120Hz display — thank God,  finally Apple! The Galaxy is the only one with a   6.2-inch display, while the other three are 6.3  inches, and the Pixel is slightly taller because   of its larger bezels. The iPhone is the only phone  on the table that has anti-reflective properties;   on the other hand, the Xiaomi is the only one  that comes with a screen protector installed. The   iPhone also has an extra action button on the left  side and a camera button on the right side, which   can be useful in some scenarios, but I personally  prefer if they didn’t exist at all, so I turn them   off. The iPhone is, of course, the only phone with  iOS, while the other three are running Android 16   in different flavors. The Xiaomi HyperOS looks  suspiciously similar to iOS in look and feel,   but that’s only the surface — it’s an Android  experience at its core. But sometimes the   similarities are uncanny, like the search button,  which looks exactly the same on both. Right now,   after doing the camera tests, the Galaxy is at  52%, the Xiaomi 75%, the iPhone 57%, and the   Pixel at 66%. I then turned off the Wi-Fi so the  phones ran on 5G on the same network and the same   brightness level. Just note that we always have to  add 2 hours of screen-on time to the watch because   that’s how much the phones used when I was taking  photos and videos outside. I played 2 hours of   YouTube on all four phones, and by the end of it,  the Galaxy was at 35%, the Xiaomi 65%, the iPhone   36%, and the Pixel 46%. I then decided to run  only one benchmark — Geekbench, which is the most   common benchmark these days. On single-core, the  iPhone got the highest score, followed closely by   the Xiaomi running Snapdragon 8 Elite Gen 5, then  the Galaxy, and finally the Pixel. On multi-core,   the Xiaomi 17 got the highest score, followed  closely by the Galaxy, then the iPhone,   and the Pixel was far behind the rest of the  pack. I would say you’re more than fine picking   the Galaxy, Xiaomi, or iPhone if you want to game  or do heavy tasks. I then put all four phones on   standby mode overnight, and when I woke up, the  Xiaomi had the highest percentage at 60%, while   the Galaxy and iPhone were the lowest at 26%.  For the last part of the battery drain test, I   connected all phones to Wi-Fi again to run Google  Meet and chat with each other. It’s not surprising   that the Galaxy and iPhone were the first to die  since they have the smallest battery capacities.   The Galaxy was the first phone to leave the  chat after 5 hours and 24 minutes of heavy use,   while the Xiaomi still had more than 42% battery  left — and that’s absolutely insane. Then,   a few minutes later, the iPhone left the chat  after 5 hours and 51 minutes. Not a bad result   considering the smaller battery capacity. Also,  as a note, none of these phones have more than 15   battery cycles and are not my personal phones.  Now, Pixel and Xiaomi were left in the chat,   but it was obvious that Pixel would go next. After  6 hours and 20 minutes, the Pixel left the chat,   and the Xiaomi still had around 30% battery left.  Xiaomi uses new silicon carbide batteries, and we   all wonder when other major brands like Samsung,  Apple, and Google are going to use them in their   lineup — or at least give us a good explanation  of why they don’t. The Xiaomi had a crazy run and   finally left the chat at 7 hours and 44 minutes.  So it’s super easy to crown the Xiaomi as the best   battery life phone of the year for compact phones  — no phone even comes close to it. Overall, the   Xiaomi 17 is a super decent phone. It’s the only  one with a proper main camera sensor, it has crazy   battery life, and if you buy the Chinese version  like I did, it’s cheaper than any other phone on   this table. The iPhone is a natural choice if  you are an iPhone user. For the first time,   the base iPhone also has a 120Hz display, and the  square selfie camera is a game changer. The only   downside is the lack of a telephoto lens. The  Galaxy is a decent choice — it weighs the least   on this table, but that comes with the problem of  lower battery life. I also feel the camera lenses   need some love, as they’re starting to feel a  bit outdated. The Pixel has a nice telephoto lens   and decent battery life, but it comes with the  problem of weight, and the main sensor is really   weak considering the price of the phone. You can  also go for the Xiaomi 17 Pro, iPhone 17 Pro,   or Pixel 10 Pro if you want to spend a bit more  money. If you want to watch that comparison,   click on this video next. Thanks for tuning  into Techmo, and I’ll see you in the next one!

title: Xiaomi 17 Pro Max vs iPhone 17 Pro Max  - You Won’t Believe How Close They Are
transcript: So, for over a week now, I've had two smartphones in my pockets, which is typical me, an Android and an iPhone. And this time, it's been between the Xiaomi 17 Pro Max and the iPhone 17 Pro Max. Both of these devices cost premium money, but one will hit your wallet a little less. Now, I've been testing them side by side within this time, and I actually know which one I'm going to pick if I'm asked to pick just one. Now, for a regular person, picking between these devices might get a little complicated. I'm going to help you guys decide which one you should pick. And so without taking much of your time, yo guys, let's get started. Vibes check. The iPhone is actually a little heavier. It's premium looking and it's clean. In fact, some people will consider this smartphone a piece of jewelry. Now, the Xiaomi 17 Pro Max is actually like the iPhone, but a little more futuristic. I mean, there's this red display which is an absolute gamecher. This display is not a gimmick because you can take selfies with the main camera using this display as your rear viewfinder. [music] That alone flips this comparison for some people. Now, fun fact, there's this thing that keeps happening to me every time I use the Xiaomi device. Because these smartphones look alike, I [music] keep ending up trying to increase or reduce the volume on the wrong side of the smartphone. And that's because it feels like I'm holding and [music] using the Xiaomi 17 Pro Max. Also, the iPhone 17 Pro Max feels a little more fragile if you ask me. I have this little dent that has gone on with this one and with about the same impact as I've done on the Xiaomi 17 Pro Max. It's just there. I feel the Xiaomi is sturdier. Did I mention these devices are both IP68 rated? So, good for water resistance, [music] little splashes here and there outside. At the end of the day, the iPhone is refined luxury and the Xiaomi is that plus a bold innovation. Just pick which suits your style. Let me know which that is in the comment section below and do not forget to subscribe while you're at that and share like this video. It helps us a bunch. Thank you. The iPhone has a triple 48 MP sensor at the rear and an 18 MP front-facing camera. While the Xiaomi has an all round 50 map sensor, that's triple50s at the back and a 50 MP front-facing camera. At first glance, the photos from both of these devices look so similar that you could easily mix them up. Like if I handed you two shots, you'll probably say, "Bro, that's the same photo." But when you take a closer look, you look into the shadows, that's where the Xiaomi 17 Pro Max starts doing its thing. It leaves the shadows just a little more than the iPhone, giving the photo that brighter, poppier feel. Color reproduction is surprisingly close on these devices. Both of them do really well with white balance. When you put people in frame, things start to split. The Xiaomi tends to sneak in a bit of that beauty filter magic. It smoothens faces, brightens the skin, all subtle, but it's there. You can turn it down using the slider right here, but it's one of those out of the box things these Chinese phones just love doing. Now, the iPhone though keeps it more honest. Warmer tones, realistic skin tones, and straight out of the camera, I prefer that for the human subjects. No need to mess with styles or filters. Now, Xiaomi does give you options. You know, you have the filters, beauty sliders, vibrant, and authentic modes. And I keep mine on authentic because I like my skin looking like skin. Still, the Xiaomi photos are more vibrant. Now, when there's color in the shots, clothes, backgrounds, even the sky, it turns the saturation knob just a bit higher than what you get on the iPhone. So, yeah, if you love that punchy social media ready look, you'll love what the Xiaomi has to offer. Now, if you're more of a natural tone person, then the iPhone is your friend. Where the iPhone warm you over with being in it portrait mode at 2x, the separation from background is clean on both devices, but the iPhone's tone just hits differently. It's warmer and more finished. But here's something I didn't expect. Shooting directly into sunlight, the Xiaomi actually handled flares better. Same time, same frame, and the iPhone struggled a bit with light control. So, that's a win for the Xiaomi right here. Overall, the Xiaomi's images are brighter, more vibrant, but it skin tones lean more toward that China market tuning, slightly filtered, and you can't fully remove that on this ROM no matter what you tweak. Maybe a global version might be better in that regard if it does come to the market. Zooms where things start separating. At 2x zoom, both are using the same main sensors. So yeah, almost identical. But once you move past that to say 5x, Xiaomi starts flexing. Colors boost, contrast deepens, and you can tell it's doing more computational photography work behind the scenes. title: Macht nicht diesen Fehler beim Kauf! - iPhone 17 Pro vs. iPhone 17
transcript: Welcome to this new video. Apple unveiled its new iPhone 17 lineup in September, including its first ever ultra-thin iPhone Air. But the most common question I've received about the new devices is which one to buy .  Is a regular iPhone 17 sufficient, or should it be a Pro or Pro Max ?  And that's exactly the question I want to answer in this video.  I have compared the devices since their release and tried out the individual features, and you will find out which one is ultimately my daily driver .  I would say we start with the design, because that's the most obvious thing, or rather, not so obvious, because the iPhone 17, the regular iPhone 17, has an unchanged casing. So you can recognize it by the new colors, which I personally really like, and my favorite is this Salba.   It looks absolutely beautiful. The changes are significantly greater , but only in the Pro model. In this case, I have the Pro Max here, but the smaller Pro looks exactly the same , it's just smaller.  This one features a completely new unibody aluminum case.  And yes, you heard right, an aluminum casing, because in recent years the iPhone Pro model has always had a different material for the frame.  First it was stainless steel, then Apple switched to titanium, and now it's back to aluminum.  This has a very simple reason, because this entire device design and housing was developed from the ground up for better thermal performance and heat dissipation, and aluminum is a significantly better heat conductor. More on that in a moment, because there are also some exciting innovations under the hood . The most obvious feature of the new Pro models is this large camera module, which Apple now calls Plateau.  And a large part of the technology is actually contained within it, so it's not simply pointless to change the design, but it really has a design-related background.  But to what extent does this new device design actually affect stability? Because Apple said at the keynote that the new iPhones are even more robust .  And one feature that should help is the new Ceramic Shield 2, which is now on the front of all new iPhones.  Normally, a compromise must always be found between scratch resistance, i.e., a hard display glass, or shatter resistance, i.e., a somewhat softer display glass.  And Apple has now said they have improved both.  This isn't just marketing fluff that Apple made up; it's actually true.  Feel free to check out some drop tests or general stability tests of the new iPhones.  That's truly impressive.  But this new design isn't entirely perfect and flawless either , because after the release there were a lot of reports about Scratchgate, that the new iPhones get scratched and interestingly it mostly affects the Pro models, i.e. the casing of the Pro models.  And one can certainly ask whether switching from titanium, which is extremely stable and leaves virtually no residue upon impact, to aluminum, which is significantly softer, was the right move.  The biggest problem, however, seems to be with the camera plateau, specifically the anodizing of the individual iPhone model colors.  I mean, there isn't that much on the Pro model.  There is silver, which is my personal absolute favorite.  Then there is a blue that is basically meant to cover blue and black as a dark color.  But honestly, anyone who wants a black device won't be happy with the blue iPhone , because it's simply not black, it's not dark blue, it's blue.  And then there's that incredibly eye-catching Cosmic Orange. And I think Apple simply took advantage of the fact that they now have an aluminum casing, where it is significantly easier to apply a color luxation compared to titanium.  Honestly, my personal workaround tip for you would be, if you're buying a Pro device, just get the silver one, because you do n't see the scratches in the aluminum casing as much as with a dark casing or the orange one, where the silver underneath shows through. Or you could just get the regular iPhone 17, because it's received some really significant improvements this year, and one of them is the display. And holy crap, Apple really nailed it here. I'm not saying I'm praising them for putting 120 Hz in the regular iPhone 17. Other manufacturers have been managing 120 Hz for years. What I find striking, though, is that all the new iPhones have exactly the same panel issues. So all devices reach up to 3000 nits brightness in direct sunlight, which is extremely cool.  All of them have 120 Hz with promotion.  The refresh rate automatically adjusts itself adaptively as needed, and can therefore even save power in some cases , while in other situations animations are smoother.  And there's also an All On Display for all devices.  And not that it's technically groundbreaking, but so far that was also a Pro-exclusive feature, and it wouldn't have surprised me if all devices got 120 Hz, but then only the Pro device had the Always On Display.  Things get a little more confusing when it comes to display sizes. The regular iPhone 17 is only available with a 6.3-inch screen, the same display size as last year's iPhone 16 Pro or this year's iPhone 17 Pro.  And anyone who wants a large display will have to go for the Pro Max, because it has a 6.9-inch screen, and anyone who wants something in between, for whom 6.3 inches is too small but 6.9 inches is too big, will have to go for the Rifen, because it has a 6.5-inch display.  Naturally, the new iPhones were also equipped with a new generation of chips, again produced by TSMC using the 3 nanometer process.  The regular iPhone 17 has the new A19 chip, and the iPhone 17 Pro has the A19 Pro, both of which are faster than their predecessors, with performance being significantly improved, especially in the graphics area .  The regular iPhone 17 has 5 GPU cores, while the iPhone 17 Pro has 6 GPU cores.  So there's no huge difference, and you definitely do n't need to buy the Pro device just for the better performance. The regular iPhone 17 is perfectly sufficient .  Especially for everything you do on a daily basis with your smartphone. And if you use WhatsApp and everything else, watch YouTube videos and do whatever else, then you wo n't notice the difference anyway.  To ensure you always have enough power, I would like to introduce you to these two new Anker Prime products.  They are brand new on the market and have some really cool features. For example, there's this wireless 3-in-1 charging station. You can charge your smartphone with up to 25 watts via Cheat 2.  Another special feature here is that it has an extra built-in cooling system with a fan.  This helps prevent the device from getting warm during charging , thus improving the battery's lifespan .  In addition, the magnetic Cheat 2 charging platform can be tilted at an angle, and there is a built-in display in the stand.  This allows you to keep an eye on important information about charging speed and temperature, and you can choose different designs for a screensaver .  There is also a charging area for the Apple Watch, and you can charge your AirPods simultaneously via the charging area in the stand .  I personally really like the design, and the workmanship and choice of materials are also of a very high standard.  In addition, the package includes a fabric-covered USB cable and a compact 65-watt power adapter.  Those who travel a lot should pay special attention now, because this power bank is the ideal companion.  A beautifully compact design, yet with a capacity of 26,000 ampere-hours.  This means you can easily take them with you on the plane and always have enough power to fully charge, for example, a MacBook and other devices.   A total output of 300 watts is possible, with each USB port itself supporting 140 watts .  Here too, there is a handy display from which you can read the most important information. You can find more information about the new Ancher Prime devices in the description. So feel free to stop by there sometime.  However, you will see an even greater difference in the front camera. And the good news first: all new iPhones have the same front camera, and it's a real extreme upgrade compared to its predecessor .  Not only has the resolution been increased from 12 to 18 megapixels, but the sensor is also almost twice as large.  This means more detail in low light, generally higher sharpness and more detail in every situation, and a wider angle.  This is especially useful if you want to get several people in the picture.  In recent years, this has always been a bit problematic. You needed a particularly long arm for that.  And the sensor is square.  And that's really cool, because it doesn't matter whether you hold the device vertically or horizontally.  You can always take photos in portrait or landscape format. And that's precisely what's so great about these large devices: when you no longer have to hold them sideways, where they 're not quite balanced, but can use a secure grip and still record a 4K video in full quality, I have to say, that's a truly ingenious upgrade that I haven't seen from any other manufacturer .  And quite honestly, if you want to take the best selfies, buy one of the new iPhones.  On the back, however, there are a few visible differences.  Two cameras versus three cameras.  How exactly do they differ?  First of all, all camera sensors have a 48 megapixel resolution and the ultra-wide-angle sensor is identical in the iPhone 17 and 17 Pro.  And even though the main camera has 48 megapixels, the sensor in the Pro model is almost 50% larger, which allows even more light to be captured.  I compared the devices. During the day in good lighting conditions, or for normal snapshots and such, you can't really see any difference.  At most, this is due to the slightly higher depth of field that the Pro model offers.  However, when it gets darker and the lighting conditions are no longer quite so ideal, then differences can be seen.  But it's not as if one device takes totally bad pictures and the other takes totally great pictures.  So the difference is really minimal, and you don't need to go to a tire shop just because of the larger sensor .  In general, I've noticed that pictures and videos on the new iPhones have less noise compared to the previous model .  And it's not due to the sensors themselves, but mainly to the image processing, which is related to the new generation of chips .  So, I think there 's simply more potential in that direction.  Only the Pro model offers a true zoom focal length.  However, even with the regular iPhone 17 you have the option of using the so-called Crop Mode .  In this case, a smaller part of the sensor is used, which increases the focal length. And that's how you can achieve a virtually lossless 2x zoom with the iPhone 17.  This 2x zoom is identical to what you get from an iPhone 17 Pro.  Anything beyond that is clearly the territory of the Pro model, because it also has the new quad zoom lens – and yes, quad instead of five times, but honestly, the new focal length is significantly better.  It looks really cinematic .  The sensor is larger.  The sensor finally also has 48 megapixels and I feel that the lens has become significantly sharper.  For me personally, it makes much more sense to use the zoom lens now than before, because the 5x zoom on the 16mm model was always just a little too close for my taste.  And well, if you want to get closer, you can also use the crop mode with the four-times zoom lens and thus get up to eight times closer with virtually no loss.  My assessment is that the camera system in the iPhone 17 is perfectly adequate for most people.  However, anyone who uses the zoom a lot should definitely go for the Proifen.  Oh, by the way, there are no differences between the Pro and the Pro Max this year. As far as the camera system is concerned, the only differences this year are really just battery life, display size, and the space for the speakers, because the Pro Max has a larger body .  But yes, "Pro" is already in the name, and that's exactly what the Pro camera features are.  This means that you can only take Pro RAW photos with the iPhone 17 Pro and Pro Max , which gives you even more flexibility in post-processing.  And only the Pro models allow you to record Progress Video with Apple Log, which is now even better than the Apple Log color profile and gives you even more flexibility while producing less noise.  Personally, I think it looks great in the grading, and I already loved working with Apple Lock and Proess before, but it's been improved even further here .  With the new Pro models, it is also possible for the first time to record Process RAW, i.e., RAW video directly in the smartphone.  However, you will need to connect an external SSD for this. Buying the one or two TB versions is also not an option.  So it's certainly an option, but you ca n't record internally with it, which I find rather strange. Why would anyone buy a 2TB iPhone for €25 if they still have to connect an external SSD ?  I don't know that. But what's possible with it in terms of video technology simply impressed me.  You can record Open Gate.  This means that not only the 16:9 crop is captured, but the entire sensor.  And this gives you another opportunity to better adjust the framing afterwards during video editing .  You can easily export a horizontal and a vertical version without losing too much screen space anywhere, and generally have the option of RAW video. You can adjust the white balance afterwards , you can adjust the ISO, and so on.  Even though we're still talking about a smartphone, and even though the sensors are tiny compared to a real cinema camera, ultimately it's a pocket-sized cinema camera that you can always have with you.  Yes, the new Pro models are more Pro than ever.  At this point, I would like to revisit the topic of the case design of the Pro model, which we discussed at the beginning of the video .  This new iPhone doesn't just look different to make it look different from previous models; it has been completely redesigned internally.  For the first time, the iPhone now features a Vaper Chamber, which ensures that the heat from the camera system and the processor is better dissipated and thus distributed more quickly across the entire casing.  And in general, aluminum is a significantly better heat conductor than titanium or stainless steel, so the entire casing, being a unibody design, acts as a heat sink, which means the iPhone overheats less.  But quite honestly, from my point of view, is that really necessary, starting with features like the fact that the display brightness isn't reduced outdoors because the device gets too hot?  To be honest, that ca n't be good for the battery's health either. And my best example was when I was on the Nordschleife a few weeks ago, I mounted my iPhone in the windshield because I wanted a camera perspective facing forward.  After less than half a lap, the iPhone simply switched off because it overheated. So, at that moment I simply couldn't use it anymore.  These aren't the kinds of problems you encounter every day, but if you're spending €1500 on a pro device where you want to use pro features, then that should be possible without the device overheating first .  The topic of charging is also very exciting, because the new iPhones can do wireless charging with up to 25 watts and MaxSave wireless charging with up to 30 watts.  So, with an original MaxSave accessory, you can charge even a little faster. But the fastest option, at least for the Pro devices, is still the wired option, as it allows for 40 watts.  In addition, USB-C is of course available for all models, but it is still the case that the 17-series models only have USB 2.0 speed .  Honestly, for most people it's irrelevant anyway.  Very few people actually transfer large amounts of data from their device via cable. Most people just upload everything wirelessly to the cloud or something like that.  And with the Pro model, you get USB 3.0 speed.  So, it's about 20 times faster.  It could still be faster for my taste. Thunderbolt speed would be really cool.  Um, with that speed, you can easily transfer even the large Pro Video files.  That means, um, with the Pro models, it's a bit faster.  And then there are a few new features and also differences between the devices.  For example, all new iPhones start with 256 GB as standard, which is really commendable. However, only the Pro version offers the option for one or two terabytes of work. The speakers have also been improved compared to the previous generation .  However, even within the 2017 lineup, the Pro models offer noticeably better audio quality, and the Pro Max delivers the best of all . And speaking of speakers, their positioning has changed.  They are on the underside, no longer to the right of the USB port, but on the left side, which makes it easier for many people to cover the speaker.  For me personally, it's even an advantage, because I'm left-handed and I always intuitively turn the device in the other direction anyway.  That means for me it's actually an improvement, but yeah, that's just FYI, because I heard that from some people who complained about it .  The prices are also interesting .  The regular iPhone 17 starts at €949.  The iPhone 17 Pro costs €99 and the 17 Pro Max costs €1449.  The jump from the regular iPhone to the Pro model is extreme.  And quite honestly, for most people the regular iPhone is perfectly adequate .  In recent years I've always been annoyed that there's no 120 Hz option and that you 're therefore basically forced to use percentages.   That 's no longer the case. Basically, there is no upselling strategy anymore.  Anyone who wants a truly high-end iPhone can buy the regular iPhone 17 .  And anyone who wants a device that looks particularly striking and impressive in terms of design can then take a look at the iPhone Air .  There will also be more content coming soon, so subscribe to the channel now .  And those who really want the best of the best, with the best battery life, the best camera system, and so on and so forth, can then pay the extra cost for the Pro devices.  But basically, you're not being actively pushed to do it anymore, because even the regular iPhone 17 was unexpectedly well-equipped.  Yes, my daily driver is the iPhone 17 Pro Max. Clearly because of the camera system, the large display, the speakers, and also the battery life. Feel free to write in the comments which device would be most interesting to you. Subscribe to the channel so you don't miss any more videos, and see you next time.

title: El iPhone 17 Pro tiene un grave problema que nadie está diciendo.
transcript: The iPhone 17 Pro has a serious problem that no one is talking about: I've had it for over a month now, and for some reason, everything related to connectivity is working terribly, and it's not just me, I'm not the only one.  To help you understand, it has happened to me several times that as soon as I leave my house where there is always perfect coverage, 5G with any phone, the signal disappears completely.  And it's not a one- time thing, it's happened to me often, both on my 17 Pro Max and on my wife's 17 Pro.  And to make matters worse, the Wi-Fi in my house appears to be connected, but it loses internet access.  And all that didn't happen to me with the 16 Pro, plus there were already a lot of people complaining about this issue after the launch and they were supposed to fix it with an iOS 26 update, but although that update arrived it didn't fix it in either the official version or the betas.  My father tells me that his 17 Pro Max doesn't have this problem, so I guess it must be a few units, but it's still very frustrating that a €1500 phone has a problem.  Has this happened to you too ?

title: iPhone 17 Pro Akku: Hat Apple gelogen?
transcript: Apple promises significantly longer battery life of up to 31 hours of video playback on the 17 Pro with SIM card slot compared to up to 23 hours on the 15 Pro, a full 8 hours more on paper.  In joyful anticipation, I immediately set the charging limit to 80%, the lowest level.  That should easily be enough for the day, I thought.   It did n't.  My iPhone 17 Pro regularly ended up in power saving mode in the evenings, i.e. below 20%.   "This ca n't be happening," I thought to myself. The 17 Pro battery should be far superior to the 15 Pro battery , especially since the latter series has already been in use for 2 years .  So I charged both devices to 80% , then unplugged them and compared them after 2 hours.  And indeed, the old iPhone 15 Pro was still at 79%, while the best iPhone of all time on the right had already lost a whopping 6 percentage points. Both models were then able to play videos for another 30 minutes. The 17 Pro fared slightly better here, losing 3 percentage points, while the iPhone 15 Pro lost 5 percentage points. Overall, this means 71% to 74% remaining battery life in favor of the 2-year-old iPhone.  Apparently I got a lemon and will have to talk to Apple customer service.

title: iPhone 17 Pro Max vs. Pixel 10 Pro XL vs. Galaxy S25 Ultra Comparison

title: I Broke My New iPhone 17 Pro Max. How I Fixed It With AppleCare | One More Thing
transcript: The scariest thing just happened to me. I broke  my brand new iPhone. I dropped it on the street on   the way to work. iPhone 17 Pro Max is now cracked.  I didn't even do anything special. I didn't throw   it. I was just a butter fingers and it fell out  of my pocket onto the sidewalk. So, I thought,   what better episode than to take you with me as I  learn one more thing how to fix my new iPhone at   the Apple Store with Apple Care. The drop on the  sidewalk didn't just crack the glass. It dented   and scratched the soft aluminum frame and the case  was also broken. So, that's a bummer. Because this   was a brand new iPhone, I was still within the  60-day window where I could sign up for Apple   Care coverage. And yes, I quickly did it from my  iPhone settings page. Apple Care is a monthly fee   to help cover a chunk of the cost from damage and  loss. After you buy a new Apple product, you have   two months to sign up for the Apple Care program.  Without Apple Care, the cost of repairs is more   expensive. And I will break down all of that for  you in detail at the end. But for the moment,   just know that Apple Care has different prices  depending on the product. I had two options for   this iPhone 17 Pro Max. Covering just the iPhone  would cost $14 a month. Or I could get Apple Care   1. It covered the iPhone and two additional Apple  products for about $20 a month. Now, in the stress   of the moment, I just picked Apple Care 1, but  I can cancel anytime or sign up for a cheaper   plan later. Next, I went online to apple.com to  book the repair appointment. Now, you can get   this done at an Apple store or at one of Apple's  authorized service partners. I picked the flagship   New York Apple store at Fifth Avenue, but perhaps  picking one of the busiest stores was not the best   idea because after 40 minutes, I discovered they  didn't have the part in stock to fix it. They had   no more displays for the brand new iPhone 17 Pro  Max. I was told Apple would contact me when the   parts come in, but I was not under obligation to  get it fixed there. So, I decided to try my luck   again with another Apple store appointment  later that evening on the Upper East Side,   and they did have the part in stock and they could  fix it for me on the same day. I just had to wait   around. I left the store. I got some coffee and I  contemplated how the aluminum scuffs on the edge   of this phone were not going to be fixed and how  this iPhone was not as durable as my iPhone 16   that was made of titanium. Back then, I never had  to worry about Apple Care for all the dozens of   times I dropped my phone. But then an hour later,  I walked back in the store and it was ready for   pickup. And I paid $29 plus tax for the repair.  If I didn't have Apple Care, it would have cost me   $379 plus tax to fix the screen. So, thanks  for nothing to the so-called Tougher Ceramic   Shield 2 glass display. And thanks for nothing  to the orange Apple silicone case, but thanks a   ton to the mystery repair technician who helped  get my iPhone fixed the same day of my drop. I   learned a ton about the Apple Care program in this  journey of fixing my cracked screen. So, here are   some helpful facts, including how to get Apple  Care if you are past the 60-day window. First,   when I showed up to the Apple store both times, no  one asked me when I broke the phone. No one asked   me when I signed up for Apple Care. I was nervous  about that. They just asked what happened and I   explained that I dropped it on a sidewalk and  they asked if I had Apple Care and they looked   up to see if they had the parts in stock to  fix it. Apple has you do a little diagnostic   test on the iPhone to make sure everything else  is running fine on the device. You also have to   back up your device to iCloud because you sign an  acknowledgement that there is a chance something   could go wrong in the repair and it might mean you  might lose your data from your device. And I think   that happened to another customer during my visit.  While I was waiting, I overheard someone get   pretty upset that he went in to get a new battery  and his phone display stopped working during the   repair process. But the Apple staff worked to help  him resolve that headache. You don't want to lose   your files if something goes wrong in the repair.  So, always back up everything at home before going   to the store. Now, let's go over the little tricks  of buying Apple Care that folks don't always talk   about. You can buy an individual Apple Care Plus  plan on any device by itself. And every device is   priced differently. If you purchase Apple Care  Plus to cover an iPhone, iPad, or Apple Watch,   it will also cover the cost from theft and loss  of your device. and you have to have find my   location turned on. But if you do and you lose  it, you have to pay a deductible. It's $149 to   replace a lost iPhone and $119 to replace a lost  Apple Watch. But not every Apple gadget will be   covered if you lose it. Apple does not cover loss  or theft for AirPods, probably because lots of   people lose their buds all the time. If you want  Apple Care for headphones, it's cheap. It's like   $2 or three dollars a month, but it's just repairs  on things like drops and spills and free battery   replacements. MacBooks are also not covered for  loss or theft. There is a value, however, when   you bundle multiple devices in Apple Care 1. It's  about $20 a month to cover three devices, which   is cheaper than covering a new iPhone and MacBook  separately. But when you pick your three devices,   you can switch which ones are covered if you  change your mind. My advice is if you are going   to hand down an older gadget to someone else, you  want to cover it with Apple Care and get a battery   replacement or fix any damage while it is still  under your name before you reset it and give it   to someone else. Here are tricks to getting Apple  Care even if you're past the 60-day signup window.   You will get the prompt to sign up for Apple Care  1 with any new Apple product purchase. So today,   if you bought an Apple HomePod speaker, you could  sign up to Apple Care 1 and then add your older   iPhone or older MacBook. It just needs to be  a device that's not more than four years old.   But let's say you just have one iPhone and it's  past the 60-day mark. If it's in good condition,   you can take it to an Apple store to get  inspected. And then if it passes, you can   be eligible to add Apple Care coverage. The iPhone  just has to be under two years old. And for iPads,   Mac, and Apple watches, it can only be up to  one year old. Oh, and what if you get Apple Care   and then you cancel? Well, the moment you lose  Apple Care, there is a 45day window that starts   so you can sign up again. That also applies  if your plan expires. That's helpful if, say,   you get Apple Care 1, but you want to downgrade to  a cheaper plan. You got 45 days to sign up again   after you cancel. I hope my little story about  the cracked screen and all the stress that came   with it helped you out. And I'm glad it had  a happy ending. Although I am most definitely   taking this Apple silicone case off and going  with something tougher. I have a few to try.   This one is from Caseify, but clear always shows  fingerprints. And I have one here that's frosted.   It's a Mag Armor Future case. And maybe I also  need to buy a screen protector, too. Certainly,   it might be cheaper to armor up the iPhone than  pay a monthly Apple Care fee, but it's always a   gamble. So, tell me in the comments, do you  live case free with your iPhone and you just   get insurance like Apple Care, or should I just  start using better cases and cancel the Apple Care   plan and hope I don't come across trouble? Share  your experiences so we can learn from each other.   And I'll catch you with more Apple news and tips  next week with another episode of One More Thing.

Now, if we compare video in low-light scenarios, the Galaxy continues with its style of more saturated colors, while the iPhone tries to provide greater visibility.  Here we also have side-by-side photos and, of course, low-light selfies.  And now we've finished with a low-light selfie video as well.  So, what do you think?  Each one has its own style, and depending on what you're looking for, you'll surely be able to choose your favorite.

title: iPhone 16 Pro Max lên hơn 30 triệu, TĂNG GIÁ do iPhone 17 Pro Max?
transcript: Before the iPhone 17 Pro Max was launched, I saw many people speculating that after its launch, the iPhone 16 Pro Max would not only not decrease in price but would even increase in price. And before that, I saw that there were a few times when people could buy it for around 27 or 28 million.  So why is there such a reverse price increase? Let's find out in today's video .  Welcome back to Relax.  [music] For many people, the iPhone 17 Pro Max is a step down from the iPhone 16 Pro Max.  The biggest reason is the appearance, the design and the material.  If on the iPhone 16 Pro Max and recent previous iPhone generations, everyone is used to the fact that its back is a seamless glass back .  So on the iPhone 17 Pro Max we are divided into two parts. Above this camera cluster, it will be made of this aluminum material.  And below this is the glass back and its color is also lighter.  For many people, this camera cluster is too crude compared to the iPhone 16 Pro Max. That material is definitely titanium, it will be more durable than aluminum. Not just a scratch, a very small fall or collision can cause it to be scratched, discolored and even dented. Somewhere less than a month.   I'm so bored with this color guys. And I think that in just a short time this color will become very outdated.  On this one, the aluminum frame is painted with a layer of color, so the color feels a bit too dark and dull.  On the contrary, on the iPhone 16 Pro Max, I find its color palette very beautiful.  It 's these neutral colors.  Well, desert titanium or natural titanium is also very beautiful and I won't worry about it being outdated or getting bored quickly.  For a normal user, I think the experience of using these two devices will not be too clearly different. However, for those of you who play heavy games, you will probably notice a clearer difference between these two machines. However, for a normal user, I think this problem does not affect too much.  There is one point that I personally find quite good on the iPhone 17 Pro Max, which is that this year's front camera is a square sensor.  So if you hold it like this, you can still take horizontal frames.  I find it very convenient.  And the Dual Capture video recording feature is also very suitable for TikTokers or content creators. I have seen a lot of videos that people use this feature to post on social networks.  However, in terms of telephoto zoom capability, the iPhone 17 Pro Max only has a 4X optical zoom lens. This one still records videos very well, even has professional recording modes. 17 Pro Max has Pro.  But I think it will be very rarely used. When it comes to actual use, I was quite surprised with the charging speed of the 17 Pro Max.  However, in terms of battery life, I do n't see much difference between these two devices.
